100→ * Each consumer in a group can dynamically set the list of topics it wants to subscribe to through one of the 101→ * {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener) subscribe} APIs. Kafka will deliver each message in the 102→ * subscribed topics to one process in each consumer group. This is achieved by balancing the partitions between all 103→ * members in the consumer group so that each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer in the group. So if there 104→ * is a topic with four partitions, and a consumer group with two processes, each process would consume from two partitions. 105→ *

106→ * Membership in a consumer group is maintained dynamically: if a process fails, the partitions assigned to it will 107→ * be reassigned to other consumers in the same group. Similarly, if a new consumer joins the group, partitions will be moved 108→ * from existing consumers to the new one. This is known as rebalancing the group and is discussed in more 109→ * detail below. Group rebalancing is also used when new partitions are added 110→ * to one of the subscribed topics or when a new topic matching a {@link #subscribe(Pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener) subscribed regex} 111→ * is created. The group will automatically detect the new partitions through periodic metadata refreshes and 112→ * assign them to members of the group. 113→ *

114→ * Conceptually you can think of a consumer group as being a single logical subscriber that happens to be made up of 115→ * multiple processes. As a multi-subscriber system, Kafka naturally supports having any number of consumer groups for a 116→ * given topic without duplicating data (additional consumers are actually quite cheap). 117→ *

118→ * This is a slight generalization of the functionality that is common in messaging systems. To get semantics similar to 119→ * a queue in a traditional messaging system all processes would be part of a single consumer group and hence record 120→ * delivery would be balanced over the group like with a queue. Unlike a traditional messaging system, though, you can 121→ * have multiple such groups. To get semantics similar to pub-sub in a traditional messaging system each process would 122→ * have its own consumer group, so each process would subscribe to all the records published to the topic. 123→ *

124→ * In addition, when group reassignment happens automatically, consumers can be notified through a {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener}, 125→ * which allows them to finish necessary application-level logic such as state cleanup, manual offset 126→ * commits, etc. See Storing Offsets Outside Kafka for more details. 127→ *

128→ * It is also possible for the consumer to manually assign specific partitions 129→ * (similar to the older "simple" consumer) using {@link #assign(Collection)}. In this case, dynamic partition 130→ * assignment and consumer group coordination will be disabled. 131→ * 132→ *

Detecting Consumer Failures

133→ * 134→ * After subscribing to a set of topics, the consumer will automatically join the group when {@link #poll(Duration)} is 135→ * invoked. The poll API is designed to ensure consumer liveness. As long as you continue to call poll, the consumer 136→ * will stay in the group and continue to receive messages from the partitions it was assigned. Underneath the covers, 137→ * the consumer sends periodic heartbeats to the server. If the consumer crashes or is unable to send heartbeats for 138→ * a duration of {@code session.timeout.ms}, then the consumer will be considered dead and its partitions will 139→ * be reassigned. 140→ *

141→ * It is also possible that the consumer could encounter a "livelock" situation where it is continuing 142→ * to send heartbeats, but no progress is being made. To prevent the consumer from holding onto its partitions 143→ * indefinitely in this case, we provide a liveness detection mechanism using the {@code max.poll.interval.ms} 144→ * setting. Basically if you don't call poll at least as frequently as the configured max interval, 145→ * then the client will proactively leave the group so that another consumer can take over its partitions. When this happens, 146→ * you may see an offset commit failure (as indicated by a {@link CommitFailedException} thrown from a call to {@link #commitSync()}). 147→ * This is a safety mechanism which guarantees that only active members of the group are able to commit offsets. 148→ * So to stay in the group, you must continue to call poll. 149→ *

150→ * The consumer provides two configuration settings to control the behavior of the poll loop: 151→ *

    152→ *
  1. max.poll.interval.ms: By increasing the interval between expected polls, you can give 153→ * the consumer more time to handle a batch of records returned from {@link #poll(Duration)}. The drawback 154→ * is that increasing this value may delay a group rebalance since the consumer will only join the rebalance 155→ * inside the call to poll. You can use this setting to bound the time to finish a rebalance, but 156→ * you risk slower progress if the consumer cannot actually call {@link #poll(Duration) poll} often enough.
    2. 157→ *
  2. max.poll.records: Use this setting to limit the total records returned from a single 158→ * call to poll. This can make it easier to predict the maximum that must be handled within each poll 159→ * interval. By tuning this value, you may be able to reduce the poll interval, which will reduce the 160→ * impact of group rebalancing.
    3. 161→ *
162→ *

163→ * For use cases where message processing time varies unpredictably, neither of these options may be sufficient. 164→ * The recommended way to handle these cases is to move message processing to another thread, which allows 165→ * the consumer to continue calling {@link #poll(Duration) poll} while the processor is still working. 166→ * Some care must be taken to ensure that committed offsets do not get ahead of the actual position. 167→ * Typically, you must disable automatic commits and manually commit processed offsets for records only after the 168→ * thread has finished handling them (depending on the delivery semantics you need). 169→ * Note also that you will need to {@link #pause(Collection) pause} the partition so that no new records are received 170→ * from poll until after thread has finished handling those previously returned. 171→ * 172→ *

Usage Examples

173→ * The consumer APIs offer flexibility to cover a variety of consumption use cases. Here are some examples to 174→ * demonstrate how to use them. 175→ * 176→ *

Automatic Offset Committing

177→ * This example demonstrates a simple usage of Kafka's consumer api that relies on automatic offset committing. 178→ *

179→ * 

   180→ * {@code
   181→ *     Properties props = new Properties();
   182→ *     props.setProperty("bootstrap.servers", "localhost:9092");
   183→ *     props.setProperty("group.id", "test");
   184→ *     props.setProperty("enable.auto.commit", "true");
   185→ *     props.setProperty("auto.commit.interval.ms", "1000");
   186→ *     props.setProperty("key.deserializer", "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer");
   187→ *     props.setProperty("value.deserializer", "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer");
   188→ *     KafkaConsumer consumer = new KafkaConsumer<>(props);
   189→ *     consumer.subscribe(Arrays.asList("foo", "bar"));
   190→ *     while (true) {
   191→ *         ConsumerRecords records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));
   192→ *         for (ConsumerRecord record : records)
   193→ *             System.out.printf("offset = %d, key = %s, value = %s%n", record.offset(), record.key(), record.value());
   194→ *     }
   195→ * }
196→ * 197→ * The connection to the cluster is bootstrapped by specifying a list of one or more brokers to contact using the 198→ * configuration {@code bootstrap.servers}. This list is just used to discover the rest of the brokers in the 199→ * cluster and need not be an exhaustive list of servers in the cluster (though you may want to specify more than one in 200→ * case there are servers down when the client is connecting). 201→ *

202→ * Setting {@code enable.auto.commit} means that offsets are committed automatically with a frequency controlled by 203→ * the config {@code auto.commit.interval.ms}. 204→ *

205→ * In this example the consumer is subscribing to the topics foo and bar as part of a group of consumers 206→ * called test as configured with {@code group.id}. 207→ *

208→ * The deserializer settings specify how to turn bytes into objects. For example, by specifying string deserializers, we 209→ * are saying that our record's key and value will just be simple strings. 210→ * 211→ *

Manual Offset Control

212→ * 213→ * Instead of relying on the consumer to periodically commit consumed offsets, users can also control when records 214→ * should be considered as consumed and hence commit their offsets. This is useful when the consumption of the messages 215→ * is coupled with some processing logic and hence a message should not be considered as consumed until it is completed processing. 216→ 217→ *

218→ * 

   219→ * {@code
   220→ *     Properties props = new Properties();
   221→ *     props.setProperty("bootstrap.servers", "localhost:9092");
   222→ *     props.setProperty("group.id", "test");
   223→ *     props.setProperty("enable.auto.commit", "false");
   224→ *     props.setProperty("key.deserializer", "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer");
   225→ *     props.setProperty("value.deserializer", "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer");
   226→ *     KafkaConsumer consumer = new KafkaConsumer<>(props);
   227→ *     consumer.subscribe(Arrays.asList("foo", "bar"));
   228→ *     final int minBatchSize = 200;
   229→ *     List> buffer = new ArrayList<>();
   230→ *     while (true) {
   231→ *         ConsumerRecords records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));
   232→ *         for (ConsumerRecord record : records) {
   233→ *             buffer.add(record);
   234→ *         }
   235→ *         if (buffer.size() >= minBatchSize) {
   236→ *             insertIntoDb(buffer);
   237→ *             consumer.commitSync();
   238→ *             buffer.clear();
   239→ *         }
   240→ *     }
   241→ * }
242→ * 243→ * In this example we will consume a batch of records and batch them up in memory. When we have enough records 244→ * batched, we will insert them into a database. If we allowed offsets to auto commit as in the previous example, records 245→ * would be considered consumed after they were returned to the user in {@link #poll(Duration) poll}. It would then be 246→ * possible 247→ * for our process to fail after batching the records, but before they had been inserted into the database. 248→ *

249→ * To avoid this, we will manually commit the offsets only after the corresponding records have been inserted into the 250→ * database. This gives us exact control of when a record is considered consumed. This raises the opposite possibility: 251→ * the process could fail in the interval after the insert into the database but before the commit (even though this 252→ * would likely just be a few milliseconds, it is a possibility). In this case the process that took over consumption 253→ * would consume from last committed offset and would repeat the insert of the last batch of data. Used in this way 254→ * Kafka provides what is often called "at-least-once" delivery guarantees, as each record will likely be delivered one 255→ * time but in failure cases could be duplicated. 256→ *

257→ * Note: Using automatic offset commits can also give you "at-least-once" delivery, but the requirement is that 258→ * you must consume all data returned from each call to {@link #poll(Duration)} before any subsequent calls, or before 259→ * {@link #close() closing} the consumer. If you fail to do either of these, it is possible for the committed offset 260→ * to get ahead of the consumed position, which results in missing records. The advantage of using manual offset 261→ * control is that you have direct control over when a record is considered "consumed." 262→ *

263→ * The above example uses {@link #commitSync() commitSync} to mark all received records as committed. In some cases 264→ * you may wish to have even finer control over which records have been committed by specifying an offset explicitly. 265→ * In the example below we commit offset after we finish handling the records in each partition. 266→ *

267→ * 

   268→ * {@code
   269→ *     try {
   270→ *         while(running) {
   271→ *             ConsumerRecords records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(Long.MAX_VALUE));
   272→ *             for (TopicPartition partition : records.partitions()) {
   273→ *                 List> partitionRecords = records.records(partition);
   274→ *                 for (ConsumerRecord record : partitionRecords) {
   275→ *                     System.out.println(record.offset() + ": " + record.value());
   276→ *                 }
   277→ *                 consumer.commitSync(Collections.singletonMap(partition, records.nextOffsets().get(partition)));
   278→ *             }
   279→ *         }
   280→ *     } finally {
   281→ *       consumer.close();
   282→ *     }
   283→ * }
284→ * 285→ * Note: The committed offset should always be the offset of the next message that your application will read. 286→ * Thus, when calling {@link #commitSync(Map) commitSync(offsets)} you should use {@code nextRecordToBeProcessed.offset()} 287→ * or if {@link ConsumerRecords} is exhausted already {@link ConsumerRecords#nextOffsets()} instead. 288→ * You should also add the leader epoch as commit metadata, which can be obtained from 289→ * {@link ConsumerRecord#leaderEpoch()} or {@link ConsumerRecords#nextOffsets()}. 290→ * 291→ *

Manual Partition Assignment

292→ * 293→ * In the previous examples, we subscribed to the topics we were interested in and let Kafka dynamically assign a 294→ * fair share of the partitions for those topics based on the active consumers in the group. However, in 295→ * some cases you may need finer control over the specific partitions that are assigned. For example: 296→ *

297→ *

    298→ *
  • If the process is maintaining some kind of local state associated with that partition (like a 299→ * local on-disk key-value store), then it should only get records for the partition it is maintaining on disk. 300→ *
  • If the process itself is highly available and will be restarted if it fails (perhaps using a 301→ * cluster management framework like YARN, Mesos, or AWS facilities, or as part of a stream processing framework). In 302→ * this case there is no need for Kafka to detect the failure and reassign the partition since the consuming process 303→ * will be restarted on another machine. 304→ *
305→ *

306→ * To use this mode, instead of subscribing to the topic using {@link #subscribe(Collection) subscribe}, you just call 307→ * {@link #assign(Collection)} with the full list of partitions that you want to consume. 308→ * 309→ * 

   310→ * {@code
   311→ *     String topic = "foo";
   312→ *     TopicPartition partition0 = new TopicPartition(topic, 0);
   313→ *     TopicPartition partition1 = new TopicPartition(topic, 1);
   314→ *     consumer.assign(Arrays.asList(partition0, partition1));
   315→ * }
316→ * 317→ * Once assigned, you can call {@link #poll(Duration) poll} in a loop, just as in the preceding examples to consume 318→ * records. The group that the consumer specifies is still used for committing offsets, but now the set of partitions 319→ * will only change with another call to {@link #assign(Collection) assign}. Manual partition assignment does 320→ * not use group coordination, so consumer failures will not cause assigned partitions to be rebalanced. Each consumer 321→ * acts independently even if it shares a groupId with another consumer. To avoid offset commit conflicts, you should 322→ * usually ensure that the groupId is unique for each consumer instance. 323→ *

324→ * Note that it isn't possible to mix manual partition assignment (i.e. using {@link #assign(Collection) assign}) 325→ * with dynamic partition assignment through topic subscription (i.e. using {@link #subscribe(Collection) subscribe}). 326→ * 327→ *

Storing Offsets Outside Kafka

328→ * 329→ * The consumer application need not use Kafka's built-in offset storage, it can store offsets in a store of its own 330→ * choosing. The primary use case for this is allowing the application to store both the offset and the results of the 331→ * consumption in the same system in a way that both the results and offsets are stored atomically. This is not always 332→ * possible, but when it is it will make the consumption fully atomic and give "exactly once" semantics that are 333→ * stronger than the default "at-least once" semantics you get with Kafka's offset commit functionality. 334→ *

335→ * Here are a couple of examples of this type of usage: 336→ *

    337→ *
  • If the results of the consumption are being stored in a relational database, storing the offset in the database 338→ * as well can allow committing both the results and offset in a single transaction. Thus either the transaction will 339→ * succeed and the offset will be updated based on what was consumed or the result will not be stored and the offset 340→ * won't be updated. 341→ *
  • If the results are being stored in a local store it may be possible to store the offset there as well. For 342→ * example a search index could be built by subscribing to a particular partition and storing both the offset and the 343→ * indexed data together. If this is done in a way that is atomic, it is often possible to have it be the case that even 344→ * if a crash occurs that causes unsync'd data to be lost, whatever is left has the corresponding offset stored as well. 345→ * This means that in this case the indexing process that comes back having lost recent updates just resumes indexing 346→ * from what it has ensuring that no updates are lost. 347→ *
348→ *

349→ * Each record comes with its own offset, so to manage your own offset you just need to do the following: 350→ * 351→ *

    352→ *
  • Configure enable.auto.commit=false 353→ *
  • Use the offset provided with each {@link ConsumerRecord} to save your position. 354→ *
  • On restart restore the position of the consumer using {@link #seek(TopicPartition, long)}. 355→ *
356→ * 357→ *

358→ * This type of usage is simplest when the partition assignment is also done manually (this would be likely in the 359→ * search index use case described above). If the partition assignment is done automatically special care is 360→ * needed to handle the case where partition assignments change. This can be done by providing a 361→ * {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} instance in the call to {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)} 362→ * and {@link #subscribe(Pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}. 363→ * For example, when partitions are taken from a consumer the consumer will want to commit its offset for those partitions by 364→ * implementing {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)}. When partitions are assigned to a 365→ * consumer, the consumer will want to look up the offset for those new partitions and correctly initialize the consumer 366→ * to that position by implementing {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsAssigned(Collection)}. 367→ *

368→ * Another common use for {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} is to flush any caches the application maintains for 369→ * partitions that are moved elsewhere. 370→ * 371→ *

Controlling The Consumer's Position

372→ * 373→ * In most use cases the consumer will simply consume records from beginning to end, periodically committing its 374→ * position (either automatically or manually). However Kafka allows the consumer to manually control its position, 375→ * moving forward or backwards in a partition at will. This means a consumer can re-consume older records, or skip to 376→ * the most recent records without actually consuming the intermediate records. 377→ *

378→ * There are several instances where manually controlling the consumer's position can be useful. 379→ *

380→ * One case is for time-sensitive record processing it may make sense for a consumer that falls far enough behind to not 381→ * attempt to catch up processing all records, but rather just skip to the most recent records. 382→ *

383→ * Another use case is for a system that maintains local state as described in the previous section. In such a system 384→ * the consumer will want to initialize its position on start-up to whatever is contained in the local store. Likewise 385→ * if the local state is destroyed (say because the disk is lost) the state may be recreated on a new machine by 386→ * re-consuming all the data and recreating the state (assuming that Kafka is retaining sufficient history). 387→ *

388→ * Kafka allows specifying the position using {@link #seek(TopicPartition, long)} to specify the new position. Special 389→ * methods for seeking to the earliest and latest offset the server maintains are also available ( 390→ * {@link #seekToBeginning(Collection)} and {@link #seekToEnd(Collection)} respectively). 391→ * 392→ *

Consumption Flow Control

393→ * 394→ * If a consumer is assigned multiple partitions to fetch data from, it will try to consume from all of them at the same time, 395→ * effectively giving these partitions the same priority for consumption. However in some cases consumers may want to 396→ * first focus on fetching from some subset of the assigned partitions at full speed, and only start fetching other partitions 397→ * when these partitions have few or no data to consume. 398→ * 399→ *

* Note: Using automatic offset commits can also give you "at-least-once" delivery, but the requirement is that * you must consume all data returned from each call to {@link #poll(Duration)} before any subsequent calls, or before * {@link #close() closing} the consumer. If you fail to do either of these, it is possible for the committed offset * to get ahead of the consumed position, which results in missing records. The advantage of using manual offset * control is that you have direct control over when a record is considered "consumed." *

* The above example uses {@link #commitSync() commitSync} to mark all received records as committed. In some cases * you may wish to have even finer control over which records have been committed by specifying an offset explicitly. * In the example below we commit offset after we finish handling the records in each partition. *

* 

 * {@code
 *     try {
 *         while(running) {
 *             ConsumerRecords records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(Long.MAX_VALUE));
 *             for (TopicPartition partition : records.partitions()) {
 *                 List> partitionRecords = records.records(partition);
 *                 for (ConsumerRecord record : partitionRecords) {
 *                     System.out.println(record.offset() + ": " + record.value());
 *                 }
 *                 consumer.commitSync(Collections.singletonMap(partition, records.nextOffsets().get(partition)));
 *             }
 *         }
 *     } finally {
 *       consumer.close();
 *     }
 * }
* * Note: The committed offset should always be the offset of the next message that your application will read. * Thus, when calling {@link #commitSync(Map) commitSync(offsets)} you should use {@code nextRecordToBeProcessed.offset()} * or if {@link ConsumerRecords} is exhausted already {@link ConsumerRecords#nextOffsets()} instead. * You should also add the leader epoch as commit metadata, which can be obtained from * {@link ConsumerRecord#leaderEpoch()} or {@link ConsumerRecords#nextOffsets()}. * *

Manual Partition Assignment

* * In the previous examples, we subscribed to the topics we were interested in and let Kafka dynamically assign a * fair share of the partitions for those topics based on the active consumers in the group. However, in * some cases you may need finer control over the specific partitions that are assigned. For example: *

*

    *
  • If the process is maintaining some kind of local state associated with that partition (like a * local on-disk key-value store), then it should only get records for the partition it is maintaining on disk. *
  • If the process itself is highly available and will be restarted if it fails (perhaps using a * cluster management framework like YARN, Mesos, or AWS facilities, or as part of a stream processing framework). In * this case there is no need for Kafka to detect the failure and reassign the partition since the consuming process * will be restarted on another machine. *
*

* To use this mode, instead of subscribing to the topic using {@link #subscribe(Collection) subscribe}, you just call * {@link #assign(Collection)} with the full list of partitions that you want to consume. * * 

 * {@code
 *     String topic = "foo";
 *     TopicPartition partition0 = new TopicPartition(topic, 0);
 *     TopicPartition partition1 = new TopicPartition(topic, 1);
 *     consumer.assign(Arrays.asList(partition0, partition1));
 * }
* * Once assigned, you can call {@link #poll(Duration) poll} in a loop, just as in the preceding examples to consume * records. The group that the consumer specifies is still used for committing offsets, but now the set of partitions * will only change with another call to {@link #assign(Collection) assign}. Manual partition assignment does * not use group coordination, so consumer failures will not cause assigned partitions to be rebalanced. Each consumer * acts independently even if it shares a groupId with another consumer. To avoid offset commit conflicts, you should * usually ensure that the groupId is unique for each consumer instance. *

* Note that it isn't possible to mix manual partition assignment (i.e. using {@link #assign(Collection) assign}) * with dynamic partition assignment through topic subscription (i.e. using {@link #subscribe(Collection) subscribe}). * *

Storing Offsets Outside Kafka

* * The consumer application need not use Kafka's built-in offset storage, it can store offsets in a store of its own * choosing. The primary use case for this is allowing the application to store both the offset and the results of the * consumption in the same system in a way that both the results and offsets are stored atomically. This is not always * possible, but when it is it will make the consumption fully atomic and give "exactly once" semantics that are * stronger than the default "at-least once" semantics you get with Kafka's offset commit functionality. *

* Here are a couple of examples of this type of usage: *

    *
  • If the results of the consumption are being stored in a relational database, storing the offset in the database * as well can allow committing both the results and offset in a single transaction. Thus either the transaction will * succeed and the offset will be updated based on what was consumed or the result will not be stored and the offset * won't be updated. *
  • If the results are being stored in a local store it may be possible to store the offset there as well. For * example a search index could be built by subscribing to a particular partition and storing both the offset and the * indexed data together. If this is done in a way that is atomic, it is often possible to have it be the case that even * if a crash occurs that causes unsync'd data to be lost, whatever is left has the corresponding offset stored as well. * This means that in this case the indexing process that comes back having lost recent updates just resumes indexing * from what it has ensuring that no updates are lost. *
*

* Each record comes with its own offset, so to manage your own offset you just need to do the following: * *

    *
  • Configure enable.auto.commit=false *
  • Use the offset provided with each {@link ConsumerRecord} to save your position. *
  • On restart restore the position of the consumer using {@link #seek(TopicPartition, long)}. *
* *

* This type of usage is simplest when the partition assignment is also done manually (this would be likely in the * search index use case described above). If the partition assignment is done automatically special care is * needed to handle the case where partition assignments change. This can be done by providing a * {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} instance in the call to {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)} * and {@link #subscribe(Pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}. * For example, when partitions are taken from a consumer the consumer will want to commit its offset for those partitions by * implementing {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)}. When partitions are assigned to a * consumer, the consumer will want to look up the offset for those new partitions and correctly initialize the consumer * to that position by implementing {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsAssigned(Collection)}. *

* Another common use for {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} is to flush any caches the application maintains for * partitions that are moved elsewhere. * *

Controlling The Consumer's Position

* * In most use cases the consumer will simply consume records from beginning to end, periodically committing its * position (either automatically or manually). However Kafka allows the consumer to manually control its position, * moving forward or backwards in a partition at will. This means a consumer can re-consume older records, or skip to * the most recent records without actually consuming the intermediate records. *

* There are several instances where manually controlling the consumer's position can be useful. *

* One case is for time-sensitive record processing it may make sense for a consumer that falls far enough behind to not * attempt to catch up processing all records, but rather just skip to the most recent records. *

* Another use case is for a system that maintains local state as described in the previous section. In such a system * the consumer will want to initialize its position on start-up to whatever is contained in the local store. Likewise * if the local state is destroyed (say because the disk is lost) the state may be recreated on a new machine by * re-consuming all the data and recreating the state (assuming that Kafka is retaining sufficient history). *

* Kafka allows specifying the position using {@link #seek(TopicPartition, long)} to specify the new position. Special * methods for seeking to the earliest and latest offset the server maintains are also available ( * {@link #seekToBeginning(Collection)} and {@link #seekToEnd(Collection)} respectively). * *

Consumption Flow Control

* * If a consumer is assigned multiple partitions to fetch data from, it will try to consume from all of them at the same time, * effectively giving these partitions the same priority for consumption. However in some cases consumers may want to * first focus on fetching from some subset of the assigned partitions at full speed, and only start fetching other partitions * when these partitions have few or no data to consume. * *

100→ * Each consumer in a group can dynamically set the list of topics it wants to subscribe to through one of the 101→ * {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener) subscribe} APIs. Kafka will deliver each message in the 102→ * subscribed topics to one process in each consumer group. This is achieved by balancing the partitions between all 103→ * members in the consumer group so that each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer in the group. So if there 104→ * is a topic with four partitions, and a consumer group with two processes, each process would consume from two partitions. 105→ *

106→ * Membership in a consumer group is maintained dynamically: if a process fails, the partitions assigned to it will 107→ * be reassigned to other consumers in the same group. Similarly, if a new consumer joins the group, partitions will be moved 108→ * from existing consumers to the new one. This is known as rebalancing the group and is discussed in more 109→ * detail below. Group rebalancing is also used when new partitions are added 110→ * to one of the subscribed topics or when a new topic matching a {@link #subscribe(Pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener) subscribed regex} 111→ * is created. The group will automatically detect the new partitions through periodic metadata refreshes and 112→ * assign them to members of the group. 113→ *

114→ * Conceptually you can think of a consumer group as being a single logical subscriber that happens to be made up of 115→ * multiple processes. As a multi-subscriber system, Kafka naturally supports having any number of consumer groups for a 116→ * given topic without duplicating data (additional consumers are actually quite cheap). 117→ *

118→ * This is a slight generalization of the functionality that is common in messaging systems. To get semantics similar to 119→ * a queue in a traditional messaging system all processes would be part of a single consumer group and hence record 120→ * delivery would be balanced over the group like with a queue. Unlike a traditional messaging system, though, you can 121→ * have multiple such groups. To get semantics similar to pub-sub in a traditional messaging system each process would 122→ * have its own consumer group, so each process would subscribe to all the records published to the topic. 123→ *

124→ * In addition, when group reassignment happens automatically, consumers can be notified through a {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener}, 125→ * which allows them to finish necessary application-level logic such as state cleanup, manual offset 126→ * commits, etc. See Storing Offsets Outside Kafka for more details. 127→ *

128→ * It is also possible for the consumer to manually assign specific partitions 129→ * (similar to the older "simple" consumer) using {@link #assign(Collection)}. In this case, dynamic partition 130→ * assignment and consumer group coordination will be disabled. 131→ * 132→ *

Detecting Consumer Failures

133→ * 134→ * After subscribing to a set of topics, the consumer will automatically join the group when {@link #poll(Duration)} is 135→ * invoked. The poll API is designed to ensure consumer liveness. As long as you continue to call poll, the consumer 136→ * will stay in the group and continue to receive messages from the partitions it was assigned. Underneath the covers, 137→ * the consumer sends periodic heartbeats to the server. If the consumer crashes or is unable to send heartbeats for 138→ * a duration of {@code session.timeout.ms}, then the consumer will be considered dead and its partitions will 139→ * be reassigned. 140→ *

141→ * It is also possible that the consumer could encounter a "livelock" situation where it is continuing 142→ * to send heartbeats, but no progress is being made. To prevent the consumer from holding onto its partitions 143→ * indefinitely in this case, we provide a liveness detection mechanism using the {@code max.poll.interval.ms} 144→ * setting. Basically if you don't call poll at least as frequently as the configured max interval, 145→ * then the client will proactively leave the group so that another consumer can take over its partitions. When this happens, 146→ * you may see an offset commit failure (as indicated by a {@link CommitFailedException} thrown from a call to {@link #commitSync()}). 147→ * This is a safety mechanism which guarantees that only active members of the group are able to commit offsets. 148→ * So to stay in the group, you must continue to call poll. 149→ *

150→ * The consumer provides two configuration settings to control the behavior of the poll loop: 151→ *

    152→ *
  1. max.poll.interval.ms: By increasing the interval between expected polls, you can give 153→ * the consumer more time to handle a batch of records returned from {@link #poll(Duration)}. The drawback 154→ * is that increasing this value may delay a group rebalance since the consumer will only join the rebalance 155→ * inside the call to poll. You can use this setting to bound the time to finish a rebalance, but 156→ * you risk slower progress if the consumer cannot actually call {@link #poll(Duration) poll} often enough.
    2. 157→ *
  2. max.poll.records: Use this setting to limit the total records returned from a single 158→ * call to poll. This can make it easier to predict the maximum that must be handled within each poll 159→ * interval. By tuning this value, you may be able to reduce the poll interval, which will reduce the 160→ * impact of group rebalancing.
    3. 161→ *
162→ *

163→ * For use cases where message processing time varies unpredictably, neither of these options may be sufficient. 164→ * The recommended way to handle these cases is to move message processing to another thread, which allows 165→ * the consumer to continue calling {@link #poll(Duration) poll} while the processor is still working. 166→ * Some care must be taken to ensure that committed offsets do not get ahead of the actual position. 167→ * Typically, you must disable automatic commits and manually commit processed offsets for records only after the 168→ * thread has finished handling them (depending on the delivery semantics you need). 169→ * Note also that you will need to {@link #pause(Collection) pause} the partition so that no new records are received 170→ * from poll until after thread has finished handling those previously returned. 171→ * 172→ *

Usage Examples

173→ * The consumer APIs offer flexibility to cover a variety of consumption use cases. Here are some examples to 174→ * demonstrate how to use them. 175→ * 176→ *

Automatic Offset Committing

177→ * This example demonstrates a simple usage of Kafka's consumer api that relies on automatic offset committing. 178→ *

179→ * 

   180→ * {@code
   181→ *     Properties props = new Properties();
   182→ *     props.setProperty("bootstrap.servers", "localhost:9092");
   183→ *     props.setProperty("group.id", "test");
   184→ *     props.setProperty("enable.auto.commit", "true");
   185→ *     props.setProperty("auto.commit.interval.ms", "1000");
   186→ *     props.setProperty("key.deserializer", "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer");
   187→ *     props.setProperty("value.deserializer", "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer");
   188→ *     KafkaConsumer consumer = new KafkaConsumer<>(props);
   189→ *     consumer.subscribe(Arrays.asList("foo", "bar"));

196→ * 197→ * The connection to the cluster is bootstrapped by specifying a list of one or more brokers to contact using the 198→ * configuration {@code bootstrap.servers}. 399→ *

400→ * One of such cases is stream processing, where processor fetches from two topics and performs the join on these two streams. 401→ * When one of the topics is long lagging behind the other, the processor would like to pause fetching from the ahead topic 402→ * in order to get the lagging stream to catch up. Another example is bootstrapping upon consumer starting up where there are 403→ * a lot of history data to catch up, the applications usually want to get the latest data on some of the topics before consider 404→ * fetching other topics. 405→ * 406→ *

407→ * Kafka supports dynamic controlling of consumption flows by using {@link #pause(Collection)} and {@link #resume(Collection)} 408→ * to pause the consumption on the specified assigned partitions and resume the consumption 409→ * on the specified paused partitions respectively in the future {@link #poll(Duration)} calls. 410→ * 411→ *

Reading Transactional Messages

412→ * 413→ *

414→ * Transactions were introduced in Kafka 0.11.0 wherein applications can write to multiple topics and partitions atomically. 415→ * In order for this to work, consumers reading from these partitions should be configured to only read committed data. 416→ * This can be achieved by setting the {@code isolation.level=read_committed} in the consumer's configuration. 417→ * 418→ *

419→ * In read_committed mode, the consumer will read only those transactional messages which have been 420→ * successfully committed. It will continue to read non-transactional messages as before. There is no client-side 421→ * buffering in read_committed mode. Instead, the end offset of a partition for a read_committed 422→ * consumer would be the offset of the first message in the partition belonging to an open transaction. This offset 423→ * is known as the 'Last Stable Offset'(LSO).

424→ * 425→ *

426→ * A {@code read_committed} consumer will only read up to the LSO and filter out any transactional 427→ * messages which have been aborted. The LSO also affects the behavior of {@link #seekToEnd(Collection)} and 428→ * {@link #endOffsets(Collection)} for {@code read_committed} consumers, details of which are in each method's documentation. 429→ * Finally, the fetch lag metrics are also adjusted to be relative to the LSO for {@code read_committed} consumers. 430→ * 431→ *

445→ * The only exception to this rule is {@link #wakeup()}, which can safely be used from an external thread to 446→ * interrupt an active operation.    451→ * {@code
   452→ * public class KafkaConsumerRunner implements Runnable {

   456→ *     public KafkaConsumerRunner(KafkaConsumer consumer) {

   460→ *     @Override

500→ * We have intentionally avoided implementing a particular threading model for processing. This leaves several 501→ * options for implementing multi-threaded processing of records. 502→ * 503→ *

1. One Consumer Per Thread

504→ * 505→ * A simple option is to give each thread its own consumer instance. Here are the pros and cons of this approach: 506→ *
    507→ *
  • PRO: It is the easiest to implement 508→ *
  • PRO: It is often the fastest as no inter-thread co-ordination is needed 509→ *
  • PRO: It makes in-order processing on a per-partition basis very easy to implement (each thread just 510→ * processes messages in the order it receives them). 511→ *

  • CON: Multiple consumers means more requests being sent to the server and slightly less batching of data 514→ * which can cause some drop in I/O throughput. 515→ *

  • CON: Manually committing the position becomes harder as it requires that all threads co-ordinate to ensure 532→ * that processing is complete for that partition. 533→ *

\n * One of such cases is stream processing, where processor fetches from two topics and performs the join on these two streams.\n * When one of the topics is long lagging behind the other, the processor would like to pause fetching from the ahead topic\n * in order to get the lagging stream to catch up. Another example is bootstrapping upon consumer starting up where there are\n * a lot of history data to catch up, the applications usually want to get the latest data on some of the topics before consider\n * fetching other topics.\n *\n *

\n * Kafka supports dynamic controlling of consumption flows by using {@link #pause(Collection)} and {@link #resume(Collection)}\n * to pause the consumption on the specified assigned partitions and resume the consumption\n * on the specified paused partitions respectively in the future {@link #poll(Duration)} calls.\n *\n *

Reading Transactional Messages

\n *\n *

\n * Transactions were introduced in Kafka 0.11.0 wherein applications can write to multiple topics and partitions atomically.\n * In order for this to work, consumers reading from these partitions should be configured to only read committed data.\n * This can be achieved by setting the {@code isolation.level=read_committed} in the consumer's configuration.\n *\n *

\n * In read_committed mode, the consumer will read only those transactional messages which have been\n * successfully committed. It will continue to read non-transactional messages as before. There is no client-side\n * buffering in read_committed mode. Instead, the end offset of a partition for a read_committed\n * consumer would be the offset of the first message in the partition belonging to an open transaction. This offset\n * is known as the 'Last Stable Offset'(LSO).

\n *\n *

\n * A {@code read_committed} consumer will only read up to the LSO and filter out any transactional\n * messages which have been aborted. The LSO also affects the behavior of {@link #seekToEnd(Collection)} and\n * {@link #endOffsets(Collection)} for {@code read_committed} consumers, details of which are in each method's documentation.\n * Finally, the fetch lag metrics are also adjusted to be relative to the LSO for {@code read_committed} consumers.\n *\n *

\n * Partitions with transactional messages will include commit or abort markers which indicate the result of a transaction.\n * There markers are not returned to applications, yet have an offset in the log. As a result, applications reading from\n * topics with transactional messages will see gaps in the consumed offsets. These missing messages would be the transaction\n * markers, and they are filtered out for consumers in both isolation levels. Additionally, applications using\n * {@code read_committed} consumers may also see gaps due to aborted transactions, since those messages would not\n * be returned by the consumer and yet would have valid offsets.\n *\n *

Multi-threaded Processing

\n *\n * The Kafka consumer is NOT thread-safe. It is the responsibility of the user to ensure that multi-threaded access\n * is properly synchronized. Un-synchronized access will result in {@link ConcurrentModificationException}.\n *\n *

\n * The only exception to this rule is {@link #wakeup()}, which can safely be used from an external thread to\n * interrupt an active operation. In this case, a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} will be\n * thrown from the thread blocking on the operation. This can be used to shutdown the consumer from another thread.\n * The following snippet shows the typical pattern:\n *\n * 

\n * {@code\n * public class KafkaConsumerRunner implements Runnable {\n *     private final AtomicBoolean closed = new AtomicBoolean(false);\n *     private final KafkaConsumer consumer;\n *\n *     public KafkaConsumerRunner(KafkaConsumer consumer) {\n *       this.consumer = consumer;\n *     }\n *\n *     @Override\n *     public void run() {\n *         try {\n *             consumer.subscribe(Arrays.asList(\"topic\"));\n *             while (!closed.get()) {\n *                 ConsumerRecords records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(10000));\n *                 // Handle new records\n *             }\n *         } catch (WakeupException e) {\n *             // Ignore exception if closing\n *             if (!closed.get()) throw e;\n *         } finally {\n *             consumer.close();\n *         }\n *     }\n *\n *     // Shutdown hook which can be called from a separate thread\n *     public void shutdown() {\n *         closed.set(true);\n *         consumer.wakeup();\n *     }\n * }\n * }
\n *\n * Then in a separate thread, the consumer can be shutdown by setting the closed flag and waking up the consumer.\n *\n *

\n * 

\n * {@code\n *     closed.set(true);\n *     consumer.wakeup();\n * }
\n *\n *

\n * Note that while it is possible to use thread interrupts instead of {@link #wakeup()} to abort a blocking operation\n * (in which case, {@link InterruptException} will be raised), we discourage their use since they may cause a clean\n * shutdown of the consumer to be aborted. Interrupts are mainly supported for those cases where using {@link #wakeup()}\n * is impossible, e.g. when a consumer thread is managed by code that is unaware of the Kafka client.\n *\n *

\n * We have intentionally avoided implementing a particular threading model for processing. This leaves several\n * options for implementing multi-threaded processing of records.\n *\n *

1. One Consumer Per Thread

\n *\n * A simple option is to give each thread its own consumer instance. Here are the pros and cons of this approach:\n *
    \n *

  • CON: The number of total threads across all processes will be limited by the total number of partitions.\n *
\n *\n *

2. Decouple Consumption and Processing

  • PRO: This option allows independently scaling the number of consumers and processors. This makes it\n * possible to have a single consumer that feeds many processor threads, avoiding any limitation on partitions.\n *

  • CON: Manually committing the position becomes harder as it requires that all threads co-ordinate to ensure\n * that processing is complete for that partition.\n *

Valid configuration strings\n * are documented here. Values can be\n * either strings or objects of the appropriate type (for example a numeric configuration would accept either the\n * string \"42\" or the integer 42).\n *

\n * Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}.\n *

\n * Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks.\n *\n * @param configs The consumer configs\n */\n public KafkaConsumer(Map configs) {\n this(configs, null, null);\n }\n\n /**\n * A consumer is instantiated by providing a {@link java.util.Properties} object as configuration.\n *

\n * Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}.\n *

\n * Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks.\n *\n * @param properties The consumer configuration properties\n */\n public KafkaConsumer(Properties properties) {\n this(properties, null, null);\n }\n\n /**\n * A consumer is instantiated by providing a {@link java.util.Properties} object as configuration, and a\n * key and a value {@link Deserializer}.\n *

419→ * In read_committed mode, the consumer will read only those transactional messages which have been 420→ * successfully committed. It will continue to read non-transactional messages as before. 483→ * 484→ * Then in a separate thread, the consumer can be shutdown by setting the closed flag and waking up the consumer. 485→ * 486→ *

487→ * 

   488→ * {@code
   489→ *     closed.set(true);
   490→ *     consumer.wakeup();
   491→ * }
492→ * 493→ *

Interrupts are mainly supported for those cases where using {@link #wakeup()} 497→ * is impossible, e.g. when a consumer thread is managed by code that is unaware of the Kafka client. 498→ * 499→ *

500→ * We have intentionally avoided implementing a particular threading model for processing. This leaves several 501→ * options for implementing multi-threaded processing of records. 502→ * 503→ *

1. One Consumer Per Thread

504→ * 505→ * A simple option is to give each thread its own consumer instance. Here are the pros and cons of this approach: 506→ *
    507→ *
  • PRO: It is the easiest to implement 508→ *
  • PRO: It is often the fastest as no inter-thread co-ordination is needed 509→ *
  • PRO: It makes in-order processing on a per-partition basis very easy to implement (each thread just 510→ * processes messages in the order it receives them). 511→ *
  • CON: More consumers means more TCP connections to the cluster (one per thread). In general Kafka handles 512→ * connections very efficiently so this is generally a small cost. 513→ *
  • CON: Multiple consumers means more requests being sent to the server and slightly less batching of data 514→ * which can cause some drop in I/O throughput. 515→ *
  • CON: The number of total threads across all processes will be limited by the total number of partitions. 516→ *
517→ * 518→ *

2. Decouple Consumption and Processing

519→ * 520→ * Another alternative is to have one or more consumer threads that do all data consumption and hands off 521→ * {@link ConsumerRecords} instances to a blocking queue consumed by a pool of processor threads that actually handle 522→ * the record processing. 523→ * 524→ * This option likewise has pros and cons: 525→ *
    526→ *
  • PRO: This option allows independently scaling the number of consumers and processors. This makes it 527→ * possible to have a single consumer that feeds many processor threads, avoiding any limitation on partitions. 528→ *
  • CON: Guaranteeing order across the processors requires particular care as the threads will execute 529→ * independently an earlier chunk of data may actually be processed after a later chunk of data just due to the luck of 530→ * thread execution timing. For processing that has no ordering requirements this is not a problem. 531→ *
  • CON: Manually committing the position becomes harder as it requires that all threads co-ordinate to ensure 532→ * that processing is complete for that partition. 533→ *

See the exception for more details\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.UnsupportedVersionException if the consumer attempts to fetch stable offsets\n * when the broker doesn't support this feature\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.TimeoutException if the committed offset cannot be found before\n * the timeout specified by {@code default.api.timeout.ms} expires.\n */\n @Override\n public Map committed(final Set partitions) {\n return delegate.committed(partitions);\n }\n\n /**\n * Get the last committed offsets for the given partitions (whether the commit happened by this process or\n * another). The returned offsets will be used as the position for the consumer in the event of a failure.\n *

\n * If any of the partitions requested do not exist, the result map will contain null as the value for that partition.\n *

\n * This call will block to do a remote call to get the latest committed offsets from the server.\n *\n * @param partitions The partitions to check\n * @param timeout The maximum amount of time to await the latest committed offsets\n * @return The latest committed offsets for the given partitions; {@code null} will be returned for the\n * partition if there is no such message.\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException if {@link #wakeup()} is called before or while this\n * function is called\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException if the calling thread is interrupted before or while\n * this function is called\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthenticationException if authentication fails. See the exception for more details\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthorizationException if not authorized to the topic or to the\n * configured groupId. See the exception for more details\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.TimeoutException if the committed offset cannot be found before\n * expiration of the timeout\n */\n @Override\n public Map committed(final Set partitions, final Duration timeout) {\n return delegate.committed(partitions, timeout);\n }\n\n /**\n * Determines the client's unique client instance ID used for telemetry.     25→/**
    26→ * The Kafka offset commit API allows users to provide additional metadata (in the form of a string)
    27→ * when an offset is committed. This can be useful (for example) to store information about which
    28→ * node made the commit, what time the commit was made, etc.
    29→ */
    30→public class OffsetAndMetadata implements Serializable {
    31→    private static final long serialVersionUID = 2019555404968089681L;
    32→

    34→    private final String metadata;
    35→
    36→    // We use null to represent the absence of a leader epoch to simplify serialization.

    38→    // initialize its value to null.
    39→    private final Integer leaderEpoch;
    40→
    41→    /**

    43→     *

    46→     * @param metadata Non-null metadata
    47→     */

    51→
    52→        this.offset = offset;
    53→        this.leaderEpoch = leaderEpoch.orElse(null);
    54→
    55→        // The server converts null metadata to an empty string. So we store it as an empty string as well on the client
    56→        // to be consistent.
    57→        this.metadata = Objects.requireNonNullElse(metadata, OffsetFetchResponse.NO_METADATA);
    58→    }
    59→

    61→     * Construct a new OffsetAndMetadata object for committing through {@link KafkaConsumer}.
    62→     * @param offset The offset to be committed
    63→     * @param metadata Non-null metadata
    64→     */
    65→    public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset, String metadata) {
    66→        this(offset, Optional.empty(), metadata);
    67→    }
    68→
    69→    /**
    70→     * Construct a new OffsetAndMetadata object for committing through {@link KafkaConsumer}. The metadata
    71→     * associated with the commit will be empty.
    72→     * @param offset The offset to be committed
    73→     */
    74→    public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset) {
    75→        this(offset, "");
    76→    }
    77→
    78→    public long offset() {
    79→        return offset;
    80→    }
    81→
    82→    /**
    83→     * Get the metadata of the previously consumed record.
    84→     *
    85→     * @return the metadata or empty string if no metadata
    86→     */
    87→    public String metadata() {
    88→        return metadata;
    89→    }

    93→     * if there exists a leader epoch which is larger than this epoch and begins at an offset earlier than
